summary:
The claim that tocilizumab is an anti-TNF antibody is false because it selectively inhibits interleukin-6 (IL-6), not tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF). This distinction defines its classification as a monoclonal antibody directed at IL-6 receptors, not TNFs.

The final summary should only contain two sentences with clear focus on clinically significant facts related directly to the query about tocilizumab’s mechanism compared to anti-TNF antibodies. 

Only include information strictly necessary for answering this specific point—do NOT add general background unrelated to the comparison between tocilizumab and anti-TNF therapy.

Final Summary:
Tocilizumab is not an anti-TNF antibody; it works by binding to and neutralizing interleukin-6 (IL-6), thereby reducing inflammation through inhibition of IL-6 signaling pathways. Unlike true anti-TNF biologics such as infliximab or adalimumab—which block TNF-alpha—the action profile of tocilizumab makes it distinctively different both pharmacologically and therapeutically within rheumatologic conditions involving cytokine dysregulation.

No further content beyond those two concise statements focused solely upon the core factual difference regarding drug mechanisms must appear here.

Final Summary:
Tocilizumab is not an anti-TNF antibody; it specifically binds to and inhibits interleukin-6 (IL-6), unlike traditional anti-TNF agents such as inflximab or adalimumab. Clinically, this distinguishes it in treatment approaches where IL-6 blockade versus TNF suppression leads to differing efficacy profiles in autoimmune diseases.
